title: What is a method
instruction:
Think of a method as a behaviour that belongs to a specific type.

A `Book` can `Describe()`. A `Member` can `Greet()`. The behaviour doesn't float around — it's attached to the thing that does it.

In Go, you attach a function to a type by adding a *receiver* between `func` and the name. Run this, then change the greeting to include the member's name and library card number.
hint: fmt.Printf("Hi, I'm %s and my card is %d\n", m.Name, m.CardNumber)

codeChecks.message: Include m.CardNumber in the greeting.

successMessage: `m.Greet()` — the Member greets themselves. Clean, readable, and the method knows exactly who it belongs to.

title: Define a method
instruction:
Write your own method.

Add a `Describe()` method to `Book` that prints: `"The Great Gatsby by F. Scott Fitzgerald (1925)"`

The format is: `Title by Author (Year)`.

Then call it.
hint: func (b Book) Describe() { fmt.Println(b.Title, "by", b.Author, "(" + strconv.Itoa(b.Year) + ")") }
expectedOutput: The Great Gatsby by F. Scott Fitzgerald (1925)

codeChecks.message: Define a Describe() method on Book: func (b Book) Describe() { ... }
Call b.Describe() in main.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Your method, your type, your call. The Book now knows how to describe itself.

title: Value receiver — reads but does not change
instruction:
A value receiver receives a copy of the struct. Changes inside the method do not affect the original.

This is fine when you are only reading — like printing, calculating, or checking availability.

Run this. Notice that calling `tryRename` does not change `b.Title` — because it got a copy.
successMessage: Brave New World inside, 1984 outside. The method changed its copy, not the original. Value receivers are safe for read-only operations — nothing unexpected can happen.

title: Pointer receiver — actually changes the struct
instruction:
To modify the original struct from a method, use a pointer receiver: `(b *Book)`.

Now the method receives the address — not a copy — and changes stick.

Add a `CheckOut()` method with a pointer receiver that sets `b.IsCheckedOut` to `true`. Call it and print the status before and after.
hint: func (b *Book) CheckOut() { b.IsCheckedOut = true }  then b.CheckOut()

codeChecks.message: Define CheckOut() with a pointer receiver: func (b *Book) CheckOut() { b.IsCheckedOut = true }
Call b.CheckOut() and print IsCheckedOut after.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: false before, true after. The pointer receiver method reached into the original struct and changed it. The book is now checked out.

title: Value vs pointer receiver — side by side
instruction:
Rule of thumb:
- **Reading only?** Use a value receiver
- **Mutating the struct?** Use a pointer receiver
- **Large struct?** Use a pointer receiver to avoid copying

Run this. Both methods live on `Book`. `Describe()` reads, `CheckOut()` mutates.
successMessage: Describe uses a value receiver — it only reads. CheckOut uses a pointer receiver — it mutates. Mix both on the same type freely, just follow the rule.

title: Methods with parameters
instruction:
Methods can accept parameters just like regular functions.

`func (b *Book) CheckOutTo(memberName string)`

This takes a `memberName`, sets the book as checked out, and stores who borrowed it.

Add a call that checks the book out to "Alice" and prints the new CheckedOutTo field.

codeChecks.message: Call b.CheckOutTo("Alice") to check out the book.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Methods with parameters are just functions — same syntax, same rules, just attached to a type. Now the library knows who borrowed what.

title: Methods with return values
instruction:
Methods return values the same way functions do.

Add two methods to `Book`:
- `IsAvailable() bool` — returns true if `IsCheckedOut == false`
- `DaysOverdue(daysBorrowed int) int` — returns `daysBorrowed - 14` if positive, else 0

Call both and print the results.

codeChecks.message: Add IsAvailable() bool method to Book.
Add DaysOverdue(daysBorrowed int) int method to Book.
Replace the TODO comments with your methods.

successMessage: Moby-Dick is not available and is 6 days overdue. The Book type now knows how to answer real library questions.

title: Multiple methods on one type
instruction:
A type can have as many methods as you need. They all live separately in code but belong to the same type.

Run this. `Book` now has `IsAvailable()`, `DaysOverdue()`, and a new `Summary()` method that calls both. Then modify the `Summary()` method to also call `DaysOverdue()` with 30 days and include the result.

Format: `"The Hobbit | Available: true | Overdue: 16 days"`
hint: func (b Book) Summary() { fmt.Printf("%s | Available: %v | Overdue: %d days\n", b.Title, b.IsAvailable(), b.DaysOverdue(30)) }
expectedOutput: The Hobbit | Available: false | Overdue: 16 days

codeChecks.message: Print using a Summary() method or direct print that includes DaysOverdue(30).
Call b.DaysOverdue(30) include the overdue info.
Replace the TODO comment with your method.

successMessage: One Summary() calls two other methods. Methods calling methods — this is how real library systems are structured.

title: Return self for sequential calls
instruction:
Go does not have built-in method chaining. But if your method returns a pointer to the same type, you can call multiple methods in sequence.

`b.CheckOut().SetDueDate(21).Print()`

Run this and see how each method returns `*Book` to allow the next call.
expectedOutput: Go Programming | Checked out: true | Due in 21 days
successMessage: Each method returns the pointer to self, enabling the next call. You see this pattern in query builders, HTTP clients, and configuration APIs all over Go.

title: Methods on non-struct types
instruction:
You can add methods to any named type — not just structs.

`type ISBN string` is a named type based on `string`. You can attach methods to it.

Add a `IsValid() bool` method to `ISBN` that returns `true` if the length is 13. Call it and print the result.
hint: func (i ISBN) IsValid() bool { return len(i) == 13 }

codeChecks.message: Add an IsValid() bool method to ISBN.
Check if len(i) == 13 to validate ISBN.
Call isbn.IsValid() and print the result.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Methods on a string-based type. You just made ISBN numbers smarter. This pattern is used for units, currencies, IDs — any type where the underlying value has domain-specific behaviour.

title: String() — fmt prints it automatically
instruction:
If you add a `String() string` method to your type, `fmt.Println` will call it automatically.

This is Go's way of letting your types control how they are printed — no special interface keyword needed yet, just the right method signature.

Add a `String() string` method to `Member` that returns `"Alice (Card: 1001)"`. Then just call `fmt.Println(m)` and see it use your method.
hint: func (m Member) String() string { return fmt.Sprintf("%s (Card: %d)", m.Name, m.CardNumber) }

codeChecks.message: Add String() string method to Member.
Use fmt.Sprintf to format the string inside String().
Replace the TODO comment with your method.

successMessage: fmt.Println called your String() method without you asking it to. This is Go's interface system at work — and you just used it without knowing. Next chapter: Interfaces.

title: Fix the broken method
instruction:
The `ReturnBook` method below is supposed to mark a book as returned and clear who checked it out. But it does not work — the book stays checked out after the call.

Find the bug and fix it.
hint: The method uses a value receiver (b Book). Change it to a pointer receiver (b *Book) so it modifies the original.

codeChecks.message: Change the receiver to *Book: func (b *Book) ReturnBook()

successMessage: One character change — (b Book) to (b *Book) — and it works. The most common method bug in Go. You will never forget this.

title: Build a library system
instruction:
No starter code.

Build two types with their own methods:

**Book** (Title, Author string, IsCheckedOut bool)
- `CheckOut()` — sets IsCheckedOut to true
- `Return()` — sets IsCheckedOut to false
- `Describe()` — prints `"The Hobbit by J.R.R. Tolkien | Available: false"`

**LibraryMember** (Name string, BooksBorrowed int)
- `BorrowBook()` — increments BooksBorrowed by 1
- `ReturnBook()` — decrements BooksBorrowed by 1 (not below 0)
- `Describe()` — prints `"Alice has borrowed 2 books this month"`

Create one of each, perform some transactions, and call Describe on both.
hint: Use pointer receivers for CheckOut/Return since they mutate; value receiver for Describe.
expectedOutput: The Hobbit by J.R.R. Tolkien | Available: true
Alice has borrowed 3 books this month

codeChecks.message: Define a Book struct.
Define a LibraryMember struct.
Add a CheckOut() method to Book.
Add a Return() method to Book.
Add a Describe() method to Book.
Add a BorrowBook() method to LibraryMember.
Add a ReturnBook() method to LibraryMember.
Add a Describe() method to LibraryMember.

successMessage: Two types. Eight methods. One clean program. You just built a fully object-oriented library system in Go — without a single class keyword. That is the Go way.
